one_line_takeaway: AGENTBAIT shows LLM web agents can be socially engineered into unsafe actions purely via persuasive webpage content (67.5% avg ASR, no prompt injection needed), and the proposed SUPERVISOR runtime consistency-check plugin cuts that by 78.1% at only 7.7% overhead.
key_finding: Mainstream web agents are highly vulnerable to AGENTBAIT: average attack success rate (ASR) 67.5% across the five frameworks, peaking above 80% under specific strategies. LiteWebAgent (72.0%) and Agent-E (70.6%) were most vulnerable; Skyvern-AI (62.6%) least. Contextual Integration (76.0%) and Trusted Entity (75.8%) were the most effective inducement contexts; Permission Abuse (80.9%) the most successful objective and Sensitive Disclosure (56.4%) the least (partially caught by intrinsic guardrails, which accounted for 72.4% of all failed attacks). Ablations confirmed the mechanisms: removing authority cues dropped Trusted-Entity ASR by 14.3% (75.8%->61.5%, Agent-E -30.8%), and an Aligned-Continuation inducement raised ASR to 78.4% (+23%) vs. Semantic Drift 48.4% (-7%). SUPERVISOR reduced ASR by 78.1% on average (largest of all defenses; AGrail second at -63.9%, Safety-Prompt weakest -35.5%) while incurring only 7.7% mean runtime overhead and a 2.7% drop in benign task completion (81.7%->79.0%). Defense was model-independent (post-defense avg ASR ~17.3% across 7 LLMs, chi-square p>0.95). On real WebVoyager pages raw ASR fell only modestly (-12.4%), and the drop was attributed to agent capability limits (misnavigation, DOM grounding errors, misencoding crashes), not defensive resilience.
core_contribution: The paper's own two contributions are (1) AGENTBAIT, described as the first systematic study of social-engineering (SE) attacks against LLM-powered web-automation agents, which weaponize contextual persuasion (fake urgency, forged authority, workflow-integrated steps) to distort the agent's reasoning and induce unsafe actions WITHOUT any prompt injection or code exploitation; and (2) SUPERVISOR, a lightweight, plug-and-play runtime defense module that jointly enforces environment-consistency and intention-consistency checks between webpage context and the authorized task goal, intercepting unsafe actions before execution. Note: prompt injection (WASP, WIPI, EIA), backdoors (AgentPoison), and full-stack isolation systems (IsolateGPT, ACE) are cited prior/related work, NOT this paper's contribution; the authors explicitly position AGENTBAIT as distinct from prompt-injection/code-exploitation threats and SUPERVISOR as a non-intrusive alternative to re-architected pipelines.
threat_model: Attacker controls webpage artifacts rendered to the agent, either by (1) hosting a malicious site that embeds deceptive artifacts (texts, pop-ups, form fields) or (2) injecting content into benign pages via compromised widgets or advertisements. The attack is purely behavioral persuasion during task execution: no prompt injection, no code exploitation, no model/training access. The agent is assumed to operate in untrusted web environments. Network-level protections (blacklists, authenticity checks) are treated as orthogonal/out of scope. Formalized as a quadruple Q = (background, goal | inducement contexts, attack objectives), where background+goal are the user side and inducement contexts+attack objectives are the attacker-controlled webpage side. Two consistency indicators define an attack: environment consistency alpha (0=inducement conflicts with background, 1=non-conflicting-but-unverifiable) and intention consistency gamma (-1=attack objective conflicts with goal, 0=seemingly consistent hidden overreach, +1=aligned). An AGENTBAIT event occurs when alpha=0 OR gamma<=0 (five of six (alpha,gamma) patterns); the (1,+1) case is explicitly NOT counted as an attack since the objective equals the legitimate goal.
limitations: Authors acknowledge: (1) evaluation limited to open-source frameworks; commercial web agents (scarce, closed pipelines) were not assessed. (2) The SE space cannot be fully covered; the task set uses well-studied tactics as a conservative basis, and robustness against adaptive adversaries is open. (3) SUPERVISOR depends on LLM-based reasoning and can be bypassed by LLM hallucination on ambiguous/text-heavy pages (three observed failure modes over 50 bypass cases: overlooked semantic conflict, incorrect permission inference on underspecified goals, ambiguous sensitivity recognition), and adds minor latency. Reviewer-noted caveats: attack pages are simplified synthetic templates (authors argue this isolates the effect and validate on 104 real pages, but real-page raw ASR differs); attack vectors were LLM-generated (Gemini-2.0-Flash), and success is judged partly by an LLM judge; SUPERVISOR itself uses GPT-4o, so the same LLM family both attacks and defends in the main config.

datasets_used: Custom AGENTBAIT synthetic benchmark: 500 web tasks (20 attack-vector classes x 5 (alpha,gamma) patterns = 100 quadruples across 5 scenarios), template pages populated with Gemini-2.0-Flash-generated attack vectors
WebVoyager (real-world validation): 26 stratified sites cloned into local testbed -> 104 real-world attack pages under alpha=1/gamma=0
WebVoyager (usability): 500 randomly sampled benign tasks for task-completion and latency measurement
Five evaluated web-agent frameworks: Browser Use, Skyvern-AI, Agent-E, LiteWebAgent, SeeAct (default configs, GPT-4o default LLM)
quantitative_results: Per-framework avg ASR: LiteWebAgent 72.0%, Agent-E 70.6%, Browser Use 66.6%, SeeAct 65.6%, Skyvern-AI 62.6%
Inducement-context avg ASR: Context Integration 76.0%, Trusted Entity 75.8%, Reward 69.8%, Social Proof 65.2%, Urgency 62.6%
Attack-objective avg ASR: Permission Abuse 80.9%, Personal Disclosure 72.0%, Malicious Download 69.9%, Sensitive Disclosure 56.4%
Authority-cue ablation (Trusted Entity): ASR 75.8% with authority vs 61.5% without (-14.3%; Agent-E -30.8%)
Contextual-integration wording ablation avg ASR: Aligned Continuation 78.4% (+23%), Optional Continuation 65.0% (+10%), Control 55.0%, Semantic Drift 48.4% (-7%)
Refusals from intrinsic safety = 72.4% of all failed attacks (597/824); timeout 8.9%, block 8.9%, invalid 9.8%
SUPERVISOR defense capability: -78.1% relative ASR reduction (best); AGrail -63.9%, Task-Specific/ATHENA ~52.6%/49.5%, Safety-Prompt -35.5%
Post-SUPERVISOR ASR by framework: Browser-Use 66.6%->19.6%, Skyvern-AI 62.6%->15.4%, Agent-E 70.6%->14.8%, SeeAct 65.6%->12.2%, LiteWebAgent 72.0%->11.8%
SUPERVISOR runtime overhead: 7.7% mean (lower than AGrail/ATHENA which require extra model calls)
Usability: benign task completion 81.7% (no defense) -> 79.0% with SUPERVISOR (-2.7%); AGrail 72.4%, ATHENA 74.7%
Model independence: post-defense avg ASR ~17.3% across 7 LLMs (GPT-4o, GPT-5, Qwen3-8B/32B/235B, DeepSeek-V3/V3.1), chi-square p>0.95
Real-world pages: raw ASR -12.4% vs synthetic, but capability-adjusted Success/(Success+Refusal) still >70%; real pages incur 1.8x latency
baselines_compared: Task-Specific defense (user-instruction layer: appends caution to prompt)
Safety-Prompt (agent-prompt layer: injects safety constraints into planning prompt)
AGrail [21] (agent-action layer: generates and refines per-task safety check items; second-best at -63.9%)
ATHENA [22] (agent-action layer: verbal contrastive-learning LLM classifier over safe/unsafe trajectories)
Excluded as impractical full-stack isolation baselines: IsolateGPT [19] and ACE [20]
No-defense baseline (ND) as the ASR reference point
